part: A Boyhood in Americus and the War
title: You're Going to College
summary: Interviewed in Detroit in July 2011 by Joe Mosnier, William G. Anderson opens on growing up in Americus, Georgia, a farming town of peanuts, cotton, and pecans about 170 miles from Martin Luther King Jr.'s home, and on parents who prized education: a Morehouse-attending father and a mother who spent twenty summers earning her B.S. while teaching, making college non-negotiable for their three children.

part: A Boyhood in Americus and the War
title: Get Off the Sidewalk
summary: Anderson describes total segregation in Americus: separate schools with secondhand torn books, a whites-only lunch counter, the segregated movie house buzzard roost, and being told as a boy to step off the sidewalk for a white man, conditions he tolerated only because he knew no other way until he resolved to leave for college, following his older brother to Fort Valley State.

part: A Boyhood in Americus and the War
title: I Drove for Horace Mann Bond
topic: Fort Valley and a Premier Educator
summary: He explains the family could not afford the twenty-five-dollar monthly tuition at Fort Valley State, so he worked as a mail carrier and cafeteria hand, and at sixteen drove for college president Horace Mann Bond, the renowned Black educator and father of Julian Bond, traveling Georgia, Alabama, and Mississippi as Bond studied education for Black students.

part: A Boyhood in Americus and the War
title: I Want Those Japanese
summary: Anderson recalls the Americus Times-Recorder extra announcing the attack on Pearl Harbor and feeling personally attacked despite living under segregation, trying at fourteen to volunteer for the Army until his mother stopped him, then enlisting in the Navy at sixteen and refusing the steward's branch because he had not joined the military to be anyone's servant.

part: A Boyhood in Americus and the War
title: The Most Educated Company in the Navy
summary: He reflects, comparing his willingness to fight for a society that enslaved him to a slave told of emancipation, and describes his all-Black Navy company of seventy-five men, every one a high school graduate, as a deliberate test that excelled and became the first Black company to integrate a battalion at Great Lakes.

part: A Boyhood in Americus and the War
title: Doctors in Six Weeks
summary: Anderson recounts being assigned to the Seventh Fleet under Admiral Halsey and dropped at Leyte Gulf in the Philippines where Japanese soldiers still hid in palm trees after the war, and how, having two years of pre-med, he answered a call for hospital corpsmen and trained for six weeks to serve as a primary care physician for servicemen, confirming his desire to become a doctor.

part: The Long Road to a Medical Degree
title: I Married the Right Girl
topic: Meeting His Wife and Martin Luther King
summary: Out of the Navy and turned down by every accredited medical school because unaccredited Fort Valley State was not recognized, Anderson moved to Atlanta with his new wife, whose brother was a close friend of a high school senior named Martin Luther King Jr., a meeting he calls prophetic, and enrolled in mortuary science because its curriculum mirrored pre-med.

part: The Long Road to a Medical Degree
title: I Met Ralph Abernathy
summary: Anderson tells of a Montgomery funeral director named Loveless offering him fifteen dollars a week and a flexible schedule to finish his degree, where he met and grew close to a schoolmate named Ralph David Abernathy, who became godfather to his oldest child, while Anderson graduated from Alabama State College for Negroes and joked about blacking out the word Negro on tour buses.

part: The Long Road to a Medical Degree
title: You Could Do Right Radio
summary: He describes singing in a radio choir at Queen Street Church in Atlanta when program director Ken Knight of station WERD, the Good Words station, invited him to audition, and he became an afternoon radio announcer alongside a Patrick known as Alley Pat, gaining the broadcasting experience that would later help fund medical school.

part: The Long Road to a Medical Degree
title: I Want to Be a Real Doctor
summary: Anderson recounts his father urging him to meet Dr. William L. Reese in Albany, a graduate of the Des Moines Still College of Osteopathic Medicine with the biggest practice in town, doing minor surgery and delivering babies as a Black doctor barred from hospitals, and how Reese's appeal to the dean won Anderson acceptance to Des Moines in 1952 after passing the entrance exams on the spot.

part: The Long Road to a Medical Degree
title: A Mortician and a Radio Man
summary: He explains how three trades funded his osteopathic schooling at Des Moines: preparing cadavers as a mortician, and reviving a money-losing one-hour ethnic-music radio segment into a four-hour nightly program he called Gala Time by offering to work for free and sell the ads himself, broadcasting until midnight and studying the rest of the night.

part: The Long Road to a Medical Degree
title: You're a Clandestine Mason
summary: Anderson describes confronting segregation in Des Moines when, a Mason since his Alabama State days, he tried to join the campus Square and Compass Club and was told his colored Masonic order was clandestine and he did not belong with the real Masons, a slight he let pass rather than risk being put out of school.

part: The Long Road to a Medical Degree
title: I Danced With Her Anyway
summary: He recounts the dean warning him not to date or socialize with white women, and the dilemma when the white chemistry department chair, after a few drinks, summoned him to dance at a school function: dance and risk expulsion, or refuse and fail her course, until he had a beer, danced, and nothing happened, a moment he calls the beginning of the desegregation of Des Moines.

part: The Long Road to a Medical Degree
title: No Blacks at Babe's Pizza
summary: Anderson recalls Babe's Pizza, a popular Des Moines hangout that served no Black customers, and how during his interview trip he stayed at the YMCA, one of the few places in the country that never segregated, which led him to raise money for the Y for the rest of his life, and notes how slowly news of distant events like Brown and the Montgomery Bus Boycott reached Des Moines.

part: Back to the South
title: He's Not a Troublemaker
summary: Anderson tells how the same dean who warned him off white women personally phoned a Flint hospital administrator, Keith Balker, vouching for him as a fine student and no troublemaker, and how the house staff training committee convened a special meeting for him until a family practitioner moved to accept him, making him in 1955 the first Black to train outside a college-owned hospital.

part: Back to the South
title: You Made a Promise
summary: He describes returning to Albany in 1957 drawn not by the segregated South but by his wife, and how a Flint couple of professors, Bird and Ginger Edwards, both PhDs who spent evenings talking with the Andersons, helped his wife hold him to his vow that once educated he would go back to the rural South that needed him.

part: Back to the South
title: This Woman Remembers Everything
summary: Anderson recounts Flint surgeons offering to set him up in practice on a border street predicting he would draw white and Black patients alike, an offer with the ulterior motive of capturing his surgical referrals, and how his wife, sent room to room by the Edwardses, reminded him of his promise and steered him back South, crediting her with every major decision of their sixty-one-year marriage.

part: Back to the South
title: They Will Be as Good to You
summary: He explains weighing where to practice as a Black osteopath barred from most hospital privileges, and choosing Albany because his insurance-man father knew everyone there and because attorney C.B. King and Dr. William L. Reese welcomed him, with King's words that the people would be as good to him as he was to them deciding it, after which patients lined up the day his office opened.

part: Back to the South
title: Just Give Them Digitalis
topic: A Country Doctor and the Trust He Built
summary: Anderson describes the inadequate care Black patients had received, examined together around a desk and asked what's wrong with you boy, and chronic heart-failure patients given weekly mercurial diuretic shots when cheap whole-leaf digitalis would have helped, and recounts charging three dollars a visit, taking payment in hams and eggs, and never turning anyone away, a trust he ties to the thousand people who later followed him to jail.

part: Back to the South
title: The Most Racist County in the South
summary: Anderson describes attending Mount Zion Baptist, whose ninety-year-old pastor E. James Grant courageously served as principal of a school in Baker County, reputedly the most violent racist county in the South where Black people jailed in Newton were never heard from again, and how he gained privileges at a Black-owned hospital fifty miles away in Bainbridge.

part: Back to the South
title: A Pistol on the Seat
summary: He recounts keeping a pistol on his car seat when driving through Newton on house calls, resolved to take a ticket but never go to jail there because too many Black people had been lynched, describes Klan rallies one block from his home with flaming crosses he could see from his backyard, recalls attorney C.B. King being beaten by the sheriff inside the jail, and notes that Black people arrested were frequently beaten.

part: The Albany Movement Rises
title: Officials Who Would Meet but Not Act
summary: Anderson surveys Albany's Black civic life, the Criterion Club, NAACP, Urban League, and women's neighborhood groups petitioning for paved streets and lights, and the white officials who would talk but not act, Mayor Asa Kelly and police chief Laurie Pritchett, both fairly liberal yet held captive by segregation, alongside rare fair employers like Miss B of Bob's Candy.

part: The Albany Movement Rises
title: They're Doing That for Me
summary: Anderson recounts Pritchett asking whether this was how he thought he could make people like him, and tells of SNCC workers arriving quietly months before October 1961 to canvass for voter registration against a registrar always on vacation and a literacy test no one could pass, then escalating to small sign-carrying demonstrations downtown that drew arrests he watched from his office.

part: The Albany Movement Rises
title: They Had Nothing to Lose
summary: From his office in the Harlem section two blocks from downtown, Anderson watched SNCC workers like Charles Sherrod take two or three people to register and get cursed and arrested, and describes them as reckless and fearless young people with no jobs, property, or money to lose, who had already slammed their wills and made out their wishes in case worse came to worst.

part: The Albany Movement Rises
title: Let's Call It a Movement
summary: Anderson tells how his stirred conscience drove him to convene the Criterion Club, NAACP, Urban League, and women's clubs at dentist Ed Hamilton's home, where the name Albany Movement emerged and he was elected president because he depended on Black patients, had made no enemies, and was young enough for the youth to follow.

part: The Albany Movement Rises
title: All Across the Spectrum
summary: He describes the range of Black response in Albany, from those who feared losing jobs and homes and stayed away entirely, to those in the middle who gave money but would not march, to those willing to throw caution to the wind, and credits a pent-up desire for freedom that Martin Luther King's example finally showed could be met without the historic resort to violence.

part: The Albany Movement Rises
title: I Know Somebody Who Does
summary: Anderson explains that, none of them experienced in protest, he turned to the men his marriage had introduced him to, Martin Luther King and Ralph Abernathy, and how the Albany Movement agreed to invite them on the condition that SCLC would not come in and take over, then summoned thousands to Shiloh and Mount Zion churches across the street from each other.

part: The Albany Movement Rises
title: He Got Stronger Every Time
summary: He recounts King arriving with Andrew Young, Wyatt Walker, and Abernathy intending only to speak and return to Atlanta, but speaking four times across both overflowing churches, growing stronger each time as Abernathy warmed up the crowd, until Anderson announced to the people that King was committed to the Albany Movement, a commitment Young later said Anderson had tricked from him.

part: The Albany Movement Rises
title: You Don't Face Down the Klan Intellectually
summary: Anderson describes the board that ran the movement, Slater King as vice president, Marion Page as secretary-treasurer, and nightly mass meetings that ran for hours, and insists the risks were taken at an emotional rather than an intellectual level, since no one weighing the bills rationally would walk into the fire or face down the Ku Klux Klan.

part: Jail, the Petition, and the Press
title: Ready to Go to Jail Monday Morning
summary: He recounts telling his three children at breakfast that he would go to jail not for any wrong but for their rights, and leading a couple hundred people who answered the Sunday call around the courthouse square; nothing happened twice, so he peeled off to his office, and on the third time around the marchers, including his wife, were all locked up.

part: Jail, the Petition, and the Press
title: She Saw Something I Did Not
summary: With his wife jailed, Anderson reflects on her: very smart, able to get him to do what he meant not to, the maker of every major decision in their marriage, who sacrificed to put him through school, grew up the eldest of four raising her siblings after their father abandoned them, and saw in him a future he could not see in himself.

part: Jail, the Petition, and the Press
title: You Got to Have Your Family Here
summary: He recalls leaving his wife in Atlanta when he first went to Des Moines with only six hundred dollars, until his rooming-house landlady Lola Reeves, seeing him pace and unable to concentrate, partitioned her single room into a living room, dining room, and kitchen so his family could join him, after which his grades rose and he found work, his wife teaching in Des Moines and then Flint.

part: Jail, the Petition, and the Press
title: They Took Her to Lee County
topic: His Wife in the Prison Farm
summary: Anderson describes being devastated when his wife was taken to the Lee County prison farm with no word of her treatment, and how the Chatwell family, John and Richard Dean Chatwell, cared for his four children while he slept in a different place every night to avoid the Klan, and reflects that one can be afraid without being paralyzed given the Klan's reputation for murder.

part: Jail, the Petition, and the Press
title: What About Our Petition?
summary: He recounts personally handing Mayor Asa Kelly a petition asking for a biracial committee, then standing alone in the city council chambers to ask its fate and being told the council had decided in executive session there were no common grounds for agreement, after which the Albany Herald printed his name and address and the threatening phone calls began.

part: Jail, the Petition, and the Press
title: Hit Me First
summary: Anderson notes that publisher James Gray, a rabid segregationist who later partnered in business with a Black doctor, and tells of the march of over a thousand people down through Harlem where a National Guardsman raised his gun and ordered them to stop, and Anderson answered that if he hit anyone in the line he should hit Anderson first, at which the guardsman lowered the gun and stepped aside.

part: Jail, the Petition, and the Press
title: We Drew Straws
summary: He recounts being jailed with King and Abernathy when moderator Lawrence Spivak kept phoning chief Pritchett demanding King appear on Meet the Press, King refusing to leave jail until officials would talk, then deciding the program was too important a venue to miss, so the three drew straws in the cell and Anderson drew the short straw and was flown to New York and briefed overnight by King's lawyer Clarence B. Jones.

part: A Broken Agreement and What Albany Won
title: No Written Document
summary: Anderson describes a second round of arrests and a closed-door meeting of three Black and three white negotiators that produced only a verbal promise of a biracial committee and a study of segregation, with attorney Donald Hollowell and King pressing for a written statement or press conference that never came, leaving King to say in the courtroom that he did not like it.

part: A Broken Agreement and What Albany Won
title: They Closed Every Facility
summary: He recounts being called back for trial with King and Abernathy before a judge whose decision was already written, the city reneging on everything promised, and the report that King had failed at Albany because he won nothing, even as Albany closed every public facility, the library, parks, swimming pool, buses, and lunch counters, rather than integrate.

part: A Broken Agreement and What Albany Won
title: Constance Motley Without a Note
summary: Anderson describes King returning with SCLC staff, the injunction against further protest issued by hostile federal judge J. Robert Elliott, and the appeal before Judge Elbert Tuttle in Atlanta where Constance Baker Motley, facing lawyers brought from Emory and the University of Georgia, refuted every cited case without a single note as Tuttle watched in wonder.

part: A Broken Agreement and What Albany Won
title: A 757 and I Can't Fly a Kite
summary: Responding to Coretta Scott King's view that the failure to defy the injunction broke the movement's backbone, Anderson argues the real break was the city's reneging, and that though Albany won nothing tangible, like winning a 757 he could not fly, it changed white perceptions that Black people liked servitude and gave Black people the courage to refuse it nonviolently, which he counts as the end of segregation as it had been known.

part: Reflection and Reconciliation
title: She Took Me to Tuskegee
summary: Anderson recalls being physically exhausted by late summer of 1962 until his wife took him to the Tuskegee Veterans Hospital to be checked and then to her grandfather's home in Sylacauga, a minister, for spiritual revitalization, and reflects that without claiming to be a religious fanatic he believes a divine plan put him in Albany, since marrying his wife was what led him to King and Abernathy.

part: Reflection and Reconciliation
title: You'll Never Arrest Him Here
summary: Anderson tells how, returning to Albany years later, a policeman tried to jail him over an alleged illegal turn until Pritchett recognized him, voided the ticket, and joked that arresting Doc Anderson would only start another demonstration, and how the two reconnected during the filming of Eyes on the Prize, dining together with their wives and remaining friends until Pritchett's death.

part: Reflection and Reconciliation
title: A Decent Man, Trapped
summary: Anderson argues Pritchett was fundamentally a decent man, not a racist, who refused to let his police beat marchers because Bull Connor had shown that brutality before the world's cameras was the last thing to do, and describes returning to a desegregated Albany with a white colleague, Rhea Hyle, finding the federal courthouse named for C.B. King and the mayor, sheriff, and former police chief all Black.

part: Reflection and Reconciliation
title: The Penthouse at the Windsor
summary: Anderson recounts the crowning moment of that return trip, staying in the penthouse suite of the Windsor Hotel in Americus where he had once worked as a thirteen-year-old bellhop, and reflects on his children, two old enough to want to march and go to jail as high school students who found it fun, singing and ignoring the sheriff, until the dangers led him to keep them with the Chatwells.

part: Reflection and Reconciliation
title: Talk to the People Who Lived It
summary: Anderson describes the visiting minority faculty series he has produced at Michigan State University for twelve years, bringing distinguished civil rights figures each February, among them Wyatt Tee Walker, Juanita Abernathy, Joseph Lowery, and Dorothy Cotton, and likens his students to doubting Thomas, urging them to come and talk to the people who went to jail and whose houses were bombed, because these were real people and not fiction.
